coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescent sensing for acidic amino acids based on the disruption of graphene quantum dots/europium ions aggregates
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qi
Song, Chan
Zhao, Ting
Fu, Hai-Wei
Wang, Hui-Zhen
Wang, Yong-Jian
Kong, De-Ming
coredata.dc:description: A simple mix-and-detect photoluminescence method was developed for the turn-on detection of acidic amino acids. To achieve this, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which emit both down-conversion and up-conversion photoluminescence were prepared by solvothermal synthesis. The carboxylic acid-rich surface not only increases the water solubility of the prepared GQDs, but also makes Eu3+-triggered GQDs aggregation possible, thus causing the photoluminescence quenching of GQDs. The quenched photoluminescence can be recovered by the competition between acidic amino acids and GQDs for Eu3+. Under optimized conditions, sensitive and specific acidic amino acids quantitation can be achieved by utilizing the changes in either down-conversion or up-conversion photoluminescence. Up-conversion mode gives a little lower detection limit than the down-conversion one. Nearly overlapped calibration curves were obtained for the two acidic amino acids, glutamic acid (Glu) and aspartic acid (Asp), thus suggesting that the proposed method can be used not only for the quantitation of individual acidic amino acids, but also for the detection of total amount of them.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acidic amino acid
Glutamic acid
Aspartic acid
Graphene quantum dots
Europium ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the prepared GO and GQDs
Design of GQDs-based acidic amino acids sensor following off–on photoluminescence mechanism
Sensitivity and specificity of the acidic amino acids sensor
Recoveries of acidic amino acids from serum sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Down-conversion (a) and up-conversion (b) PL spectra of GQDs under different conditions. [GQDs]=0.3mg/mL, [Eu3+]=0.1mM, [Glu]=[Asp]=2mM.
AFM images (left) and height profiles (right )of (a) free GQDs, (b) GQDs+Eu3+ and (c) GQDs+Eu3++Glu.
HRTEM (up) and DLS (down) characterization of GQDs in different conditions. The figures (left to right) represent GQDs, GQDs+Eu3+, GQDs+Eu3++Glu, respectively.

Sensitivity of the GQDs-based Glu sensor using up-conversion mode. (a) Glu concentration-dependent change in the PL spectra of GQDs. (b) Glu concentration-dependent PL intensity change at λ=443nm. The insert shows the PL change in the Glu concentration range of 1–200μM, the solid line represents a linear fit to the data. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

Selectivity of the acidic amino acids-sensing system using up-conversion mode. [Glu]=[Asp]=0.2mM, the concentrations of other amino acids were all 2mM. The results of down-conversion mode were shown in Fig. S18.
The working mechanism of GQDs-based photoluminescent sensors for Glu and Asp detection.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent sensing for acidic amino acids based on the disruption of graphene quantum dots/europium ions aggregates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Research Centre for Analytical Sciences, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioactive Materials (Ministry of Education), College of Life Sciences, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioactive Materials (Ministry of Education), College of Life Sciences, Nankai University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, P.R. China. Fax: +86 22 23502458.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple mix-and-detect photoluminescence method was developed for the turn-on detection of acidic amino acids. To achieve this, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which emit both down-conversion and up-conversion photoluminescence were prepared by solvothermal synthesis. The carboxylic acid-rich surface not only increases the water solubility of the prepared GQDs, but also makes Eu3+-triggered GQDs aggregation possible, thus causing the photoluminescence quenching of GQDs. The quenched photoluminescence can be recovered by the competition between acidic amino acids and GQDs for Eu3+. Under optimized conditions, sensitive and specific acidic amino acids quantitation can be achieved by utilizing the changes in either down-conversion or up-conversion photoluminescence. Up-conversion mode gives a little lower detection limit than the down-conversion one. Nearly overlapped calibration curves were obtained for the two acidic amino acids, glutamic acid (Glu) and aspartic acid (Asp), thus suggesting that the proposed method can be used not only for the quantitation of individual acidic amino acids, but also for the detection of total amount of them.

ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids are building blocks of proteins. Due to the biological importance of amino acids, there has been a continued interest in the development of reliable methods for the determination of amino acids for diagnostic and research purposes (Meesters, 2013; Ou et al., 2013; Evans et al., 2012; Zhou and Yoon, 2012; Wang and Wu et al., 2008; Zhou et al., 2010; Jia et al., 2011; Guo et al., 2010). Acidic amino acids, including glutamic acid (Glu) and aspartic acid (Asp), have two free carboxylic acid groups instead of one. Acidic amino acids are usually found on the surface of water-soluble proteins and play important roles in various biological progresses (Balcar, 2002; Ren et al., 2013; Chu et al., 2011; Samways and Egan, 2007). For example, Glu is the most abundant excitatory neurotransmitter in the central nervous system (Meldrum, 2000), and high concentration of Glu is found in cerebral ischemia injury and some neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease (AD), vascular dementia and so on (Brown and Nijjar, 1995; Mo et al., 2011). Asp is key to a healthy metabolism. It has a crucial role in producing energy, and proper levels of Asp are necessary for the synthesis of other biochemicals and amino acids (Galili, 2011).

The commonly used analytical methods for Glu and Asp detection are based on chromatographic technique such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), cation-exchange chromatography and micellar electrokinetic chromatography (MEKC) (Armenta et al., 2009; Rebane and Herodes, 2010; Chen et al., 2014; Yan et al., 2014). Microchip electrophoresis (MCE) and microfluidic techniques are also reported (Li et al., 2013; Cellar et al., 2005). Although sensitive, they are costly, time-consuming or require sophisticated instruments. Therefore, cost-effective, simple, rapid and sensitive detection platforms for acidic amino acids are highly desired. Photoluminescence (PL) analysis might be a good choice due to high sensitivity, selectivity and most commonly used instruments.

Recently, graphene and its derivatives have received much attention for their many unique properties (Myung et al., 2011; Hu et al., 2010; Geim, 2009), such as excellent chemical stability, environmental friendliness, water dispersibility, good biocompatibility and so on. Compared to two-dimensional graphene sheets, zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which consist of graphene nanosheets in small sizes (Ponomarenko et al., 2008), possess strong quantum confinement and edge effects (Zhuo et al., 2012). GQDs hold great promise as a new class of fluorophores owing to their excellent photostability, good biocompatibility, low cell toxicity and tunable PL properties (Sun et al., 2013; Qian et al., 2014). Several groups have demonstrated that GQDs can be used in various applications such as photovoltaics, bioimaging, biosensing and so on (Zheng et al., 2013; Ren et al., 2010). However, the development and application of GQDs are still in early stage and more works need to be done.

Some effective efforts have been done to improve the PL properties of GQDs. For example, Pan et al. have developed a hydrothermal route to cut preoxidized graphene sheets into GQDs with blue emission (Pan et al., 2010). Shen et al. proposed a surface-passivated method to fabricate of GQDs with frequency up-converted emission (Shen et al., 2011). Using similar methods, Zhuo et al. got GQDs with excitation-independent down-conversion and up-conversion PL behaviours (Zhuo et al., 2012). Up-conversion PL refers to nonlinear optical processes, in which the sequential absorption of two or more photons leads to the emission of light at shorter wavelength than the excitation wavelength. Compared to traditional down-conversion PL, up-conversion PL has higher photochemical stability, better tissue penetration ability, and is free of background auto-fluorescence (Mai et al., 2007; Yi and Chow, 2006; Yu et al., 2009). Most studies on up-conversion PL focus on lanthanide-doped up-conversion nanoparticles, but water-soluble up-conversion nanoparticles are not easy to be achieved. Therefore, hydrophilic GQDs with up-conversion PL have become an active area of interest. Many groups have got GQDs with up-conversion luminescence (Shen et al., 2012; Cheng et al., 2010). But as far as we know, the applications of the up-conversion property of GQDs were rarely reported.

Herein, water-soluble GQDs, which emit both down-conversion and up-conversion PL, were prepared, and a simple mix-and-detect strategy was designed for acidic amino acids quantitation by utilizing the competition between acidic amino acids and carboxylic acid moieties on the prepared GQDs surface for Eu3+. The results showed that both down-conversion and up-conversion PL could be used for sensitive and specific detection of acidic amino acids, and a relatively better result was given by up-conversion PL mode.

Graphite powder (325 meshes) was purchased from Baichuan Graphite Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). Eu(NO3)3, Gd(NO3)3, Tm(NO3)3, Tb(NO3)3, Lu(NO3)3 (99.99%) was provided from Alfa Aesar. Amino acids were obtained from Sangon Biotech. Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The used metal salts (Hg(Ac)2, Mg(NO3)2, Mn(Ac)2, Zn(Ac)2, AgNO3, Ni(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)2, Co(Ac)2, Cd(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, Ca(Ac)2 and Pb(NO3)2), ascorbic acid, propane diacid, oxalic acid and dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Sigma. Fetal bovine serum sample was purchased from Gibco. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummers' method (Hummers and Offeman, 1958; Zhang and Kong, 2013). The details can be found in supporting information. The prepared GO (0.1g) was oxidized in 80mL mixture of concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3 (v/v=3:1) for 12h under mild ultrasonication (500W, 100kHZ). After addition of 600mL deionized water, the mixture was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 30min. The obtained dark brown precipitates were redissolved in deionized water, and the solution was centrifuged again to remove remaining H2SO4 and HCl. The obtained brown solid was sonicated in 100mL of water, and NH3∙H2O was added to adjust the pH to neutral. The suspension was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethlene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100mL) and heated at 180°C for 8h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting black solution was filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane to obtain brown suspension. The colloidal solution was further dialyzed in dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) for 24h and GQDs were obtained.

30μL GQDs (1mg/mL) was mixed with 5μL glycine–HCl buffer (50mM, pH 3.4). After addition 0.1mM Eu3+ (final concentration) and different amounts of Glu or Asp, the volume of the mixture was adjusted to 100μL with deionized water. Then, the mixture was incubated at 37°C for 2h. The PL spectrum of the resulting mixture was recorded. The excitation wavelengths were set at 315 and 650nm, down-conversion and up-conversion PL were recorded, respectively. The PL intensity at 443nm was used for the quantitative detection of Glu or/and Asp. Excitation and emission slit widths were both set to 5nm for down-conversion PL detection, and set to 10nm for up-conversion PL detection. The optical path length of the quartz cell was 1.0cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the prepared GO and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The details about synthesis and characterization of GO, the synthetic precursor of GQDs, can be found in supporting information (Fig. S1). The luminescent GQDs were obtained by a oxidation–hydrothermal synthesis–seperation process (Jin et al., 2013). Fig. S2a presents the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs, in which the characteristic absorption bands of GQDs, such as stretching vibrations of C–OH at 3453cm−1, C–O at 1114cm−1 and C=C at 1628cm−1, were observed (Zheng et al., 2013). Fig. S2b presents the Raman spectra of the prepared GO and GQDs. These spectra have two typical peaks at about 1344cm−1 and 1580cm−1, which correspond to D band and G band, respectively. G band is related to sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, and D band arises from structural defects created by the attachment of oxygen-containing groups on the basal plane of graphene (Wang et al., 2008). The intensity ratio of I(D)/I(G) is an essential parameter to characterize the disorder degree of graphene materials. The prepared GO had a I(D)/I(G) ratio of 0.94, which increased to 1.52 for GODs. The increase of oxidation degree indicates that GQDs contain more oxygen-containing groups (epoxy, –OH and –COOH) on the surface, thus possessing good water solubility and the capability of coordinating with Eu3+.

The optical properties of GQDs were shown in Fig. S3. The UV–vis absorption band nearly covers the whole UV region, and no clear absorption peak was observed. Correspondingly, the blue-luminescent GQDs has symmetrical and broad down-conversion PL peak, whose wavelength and intensity are dependent on excitation wavelength. The PL peak shifts to longer wavelength with increasing the excitation wavelength from 310nm to 430nm, and the strongest PL emission peak was observed at 435nm when excited at 315nm. Besides the strong down-conversion PL properties, GQDs show a clear up-conversion PL feature. The up-conversion PL of the prepared GQDs also shows an excitation-dependent behavior, i.e. a redshift of the emission wavelength was observed with the increase of excitation wavelength in the range of 600–800nm. The strongest up-conversion PL emission peak was also observed at 435nm when 650nm was used as the excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of GQDs-based acidic amino acids sensor following off–on photoluminescence mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The design strategy of GQDs-based acidic amino acids sensor is shown in Scheme 1. The prepared GQDs with intense PL were used. Addition of Eu3+ could induce the aggregation of GQDs due to the strong coordination between Eu3+ and the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of GQDs (Bai et al., 2013; Zhao et al., 2011; Samy et al., 2010). As a result, the PL of GQDs is quenched by energy transfer or/and electron transfer (PL turn-off). Lanthanide ions are hard acceptors which can coordinate strongly with the unprotonated carboxylate oxygen of amino acids (Dickins et al., 2004). Acidic amino acids, such as Glu and Asp, contain two carboxylic acid groups, which could synergistically coordinate with Eu3+. In addition, the amino nitrogen of the amino acids might also participate in the coordination (Dickins et al., 2004). As a result, acidic amino acids might display a higher affinity for Eu3+ than GQDs. Therefore, in the presence of Glu or/and Asp, Eu3+ will preferentially bind with the acidic amino acids, leading to the release of Eu3+ from the surface of GQDs and the disruption of GQDs/Eu3+ aggregates. As a result, the PL of GQDs recovers (PL turn-on). By following the change in GQDs PL, the quantitation of acidic amino acids can be achieved.

The results in Fig. 1 demonstrated the proposed mechanism. Free GQDs emitted strong down-conversion and up-conversion PL. When Eu3+ was introduced to the GQDs solution, the PL intensity was greatly quenched due to the formation of GQDs/Eu3+ aggregates. The quenching efficiency was over 92%. Subsequent addition of Glu or Asp caused the recovery of quenched PL, accompanied by the redshift of the PL emission wavelength from 435nm to 443nm. The shift of emission wavelength might suggest that Eu3+-induced close attachment of GQDs affects the luminescence property of GQDs to some degree. We attribute the PL recovery in the presence of Glu or Asp to synergistical coordination of two carboxylic acid groups with Eu3+. To demonstrate this, the abilities of three organic acids to recover the PL of GQDs/Eu3+ aggregates were compared (Fig. S5). Oxalic acid and propane diacid, which have two carboxylic acid groups, could effectively recover the quenched PL. However, the acid without such two carboxylic acid groups, for example ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) could not work.

To further demonstrate the proposed mechanism, the morphology and thickness changes of GQDs after sequential addition of Eu3+ and Glu were investigated by AFM ( Fig. 2). Before Eu3+ treatment, GQDs with good dispersibility and narrow size distribution could be observed, the height of GQDs was about 1.335nm, which suggested that most of GQDs are single-layered or bilayered. In the presence of Eu3+, the nanoparticles aggregated together, and a cluster of particles with unchanged height but bigger particle sizes appeared in the AFM image. With the addition of acidic amino acid Glu, however, GQDs/Eu3+ aggregates dissociated and monodisperse GQD nanoparticles were observed again. The insets in Fig. 2 also provided the macroscopic evidence of the changed diameter and morphology. Red-brown precipitates could be found with the addition of Eu3+ into GQDs solution, and the precipitates disappeared with the further addition of Glu. These results confirm the proposed mechanism, i.e. Eu3+ induces the aggregation of GQDs, acidic amino acids can disrupt the aggregates.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to validate the Eu3+-induced aggregation of GQDs by recording the changes in hydrodynamic diameter (D h) of GQDs before and after Eu3+ addition. As shown in Fig. 3, with the addition of Eu3+, the average D h of GQDs increased from 23 to 180nm, providing a strong indication that monodisperse GQDs assembled into aggregates. Then, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used to examine the morphology change in different conditions. the HRTEM images of GQDs showed characteristic monodisperse particle sizes and shapes. The diameter of GQDs was estimated to be about 15nm. Introduction of Eu3+ led to an obvious increase in the particle size due to the aggregation of nanoparticles. Correspondingly, the morphology of the aggregates clearly revealed the close stacking of many GQD nanoparticles. With the addition of Glu, the aggregates of GQDs were disrupted and the diameter decreased to about 24nm, which closely corresponds to the result of the DLS.

The quenching abilities of several metal ions to GQDs PL were compared. The tested metal ions include common metal ions such as Mg2+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Hg2+ and rare earth ions such as Eu3+, Gd3+, Tm3+, Tb3+, Lu3+. None of the common metal ions could efficiently quench the PL of GQDs (Fig. S6). However, all of the five rare earth ions displayed the ability to quench the PL of GQDs. Eu3+ showed the highest quenching efficiency, and Tb3+ give the second one (Fig. S7). This result demonstrated that Eu3+ is a good candidate PL quencher for the design of acidic amino acids sensor.

Eu3+ concentration was carefully selected to ensure that the PL of GQDs could be quenched efficiently in the absence of acidic amino acids, and significant PL recovery could be observed with acidic amino acids addition. As expected, the PL intensity of GQDs significantly decreased with increasing Eu3+ concentration (Fig. S8). Over 92% of the PL was quenched by the addition of 100μM Eu3+. The quenching efficiency could reach over 95% when 250μM Eu3+ was added. Addition of acidic amino acids could recover the PL of the sensing system. The I/I 0, which represents the PL intensity ratio in the presence (I) and absence (I 0) of acidic amino acids, was used as the criteria to select optimal Eu3+ concentration. As shown in Fig. S9 and S10, in the low concentration range, the I/I 0 value increased with Eu3+ concentration, and gave a maximum at the Eu3+ concentration of 100μM. After that, the I/I 0 value decreased with Eu3+ concentration due to the presence of excess free Eu3+ ions, which would preferentially coordinate with acidic amino acids. Therefore, 100μM Eu3+ was selected in subsequent experiments. This Eu3+ concentration was suitable for both down-conversion and up-conversion detection modes.

pH-dependence of the PL behavior of free GQDs was evaluated firstly. The luminescence intensity of free GQDs remained unchanged from pH 3.0 to 9.0 (Fig. S11a), indicating that the PL of GQDs is stable in this range and GQDs can be used for sensor design in a wide pH range. Then, pH-dependence of GQDs-based acidic amino acids sensor was evaluated. In the pH range of 3.2–7.0, the PL quenching of GQDs by Eu3+ was nearly unaffected (Fig. S11b). However, with the further decrease of pH (for example at the pH of 3.0), the PL quenching ability of Eu3+ to GQDs sharply decreased. This might be attributed to the protonation of the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of GQDs. At pH of 3.0, the surface of GQDs is mainly positive charges with a zeta potential of 0.99mV, the electrostatic repulsion between GQDs and Eu3+ impairs the coordination reaction between them. Addition of acidic amino acids could cause the recovery of PL intensity, more obvious PL recovery was observed in relative low pH range. This might be attributed to the increase of binding affinity between GQDs and Eu3+ at high pH range, which certainly brings adverse effects on the competition between acidic amino acids and GQDs for Eu3+. With the increase of pH, the surface charges of GQDs became more and more negative, and the zeta potentials of GQDs decreased to −1.99mV for pH 3.4 and −27.1mV for pH 5.0. The highest signal-to-background ratio was obtained at pH 3.4 (Fig. S12). As a result, Gly–HCl buffer with pH of 3.4 was used in the sensing platform of acidic amino acids.

Eu3+-induced formation of GQD aggregates was able to finish in a very short time. With the addition of Eu3+, the PL of GQDs was quenched rapidly and reached its minimum value in 2min. The formed GQDs/Eu3+ aggregates were very stable and no detectable changes were observed for the quenched PL in several days. A relatively long time was needed to reach equilibrium for the competition between acidic amino acids and GQDs for binding with Eu3+, but the PL recovery could also reach a plateau in 2h (Fig. S13), and the obtained PL intensity could remain stable for more than one day. These results implied that this simple mix-and-detect acidic amino acids detection strategy had not strict time constraints. Then, we chose 2h as the reaction time in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and specificity of the acidic amino acids sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimized conditions, the feasibility of this method for Glu and Asp detection was investigated using both down-conversion and up-conversion PL modes. Encouragingly, we observed that PL signal of the sensing system was highly Glu or Asp concentration-dependent, and similar linear detection ranges were observed in both detection modes ( Figs. 4, S14–S16). Fig. 4 gave the representative results of Glu detection by utilizing up-conversion PL of GQDs. The PL intensity significantly increased with the addition of Glu in the low concentration range, and nearly reached a plateau with 1.5mM Glu. A linear relationship (R
2 =0.9961) was observed over the range of 1~200μM. The linear regression equation was I=38.7+733.1C Glu (mM). The detection limit, calculated as the 3δ/slope of the calibration curve (δ: standard deviation of blank samples) was 0.19μM, which is comparable to or even lower than those of some reported methods using sophisticated instruments (Table S1) (Li et al., 2013; Fa et al., 2013; Naval, 2006; Shi et al., 2008). Down-conversion mode gave a same linear detection range with a detection limit of 0.32μM, which is a little higher than that of up-conversion mode. This might be attributed to the less interference from background in up-conversion mode. Under the same conditions, similar results were obtained for Asp detection. That is, Asp quantitation could be achieved in the linear detection range of 1–220μM, with detection limits of 0.18μM and 0.33μM for up-conversion and down-conversion detection modes, respectively. It should be noted that the proposed method gave nearly identical detection sensitivities for Glu and Asp, their calibration curves were nearly overlapped (Fig. S17), thus suggesting that this method can be used not only for the quantitation of either Glu or Asp, but also for the detection of total concentration of these two acidic amino acids.

The specificity of this method was investigated by testing the response of the sensing system to other 18 amino acids with 10 times concentration of Glu or Asp. Of these competitive amino acids, none could give obvious increase of PL intensity ( Figs. 5 and S18), demonstrating high selectivity of this sensor for Glu and Asp detection. Such high selectivity may be attributed to the synergistical coordination between Eu3+ and the two carboxylic acid groups of acidic amino acids. To demonstrate this, the binding constants of Eu3+ with Glu or lysine (Lys), which contains only one carboxylic acid group, were compared. The results showed that the binding constant of Eu3+ with Glu was about 60 times higher than that of Eu3+ with Lys (3204±947 vs 56.65±16.18M−1) (Fig. S19). Because phosphates and EDTA can also coordinate with Eu3+, PL recovery was also observed in the presence of them (Fig. S4). Therefore, phosphates and EDTA must be removed or masked in some practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recoveries of acidic amino acids from serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: To study the feasibility of this method for Glu or/and Asp quantitation in real samples, three concentrations of Glu or Asp (50μM, 100μM, 150μM) were added to a fetal bovine serum sample, and the PL intensities were read to obtain the recovery values from the calibration graph. The recoveries of added Glu and Asp were in the range of 98.2–103.9% (Table S2). Other 18 amino acids with total concentration of 0.18mM were added into the serum sample to construct a standard amino acids solution. The recoveries of added Glu and Asp from this standard solution were in the range of 98.4–102.5% (Table S3). These results indicated that this method might detect Glu and Asp in real samples with little interference.

In summary, water-soluble GQDs, which emit both down-conversion and up-conversion PL, were synthesized, and a simple mix-and-detect method was developed for the quantitative detection of acidic amino acids on the basis of PL quenching caused by Eu3+-triggered aggregation of GQDs and subsequent PL recovery due to the competition between acidic amino acids and GQDs for Eu3+. Both the down-conversion and up-conversion detection modes can achieve the sensitive and specific detection of acidic amino acids. Up-conversion mode gives a littler lower detection limit than the down-conversion one. Nearly overlapped calibration curves were obtained for the two acidic amino acids, Glu and Asp, thus suggesting that the proposed method can be used not only for the quantitation of individual acidic amino acids, but also for the detection of total concentration of them.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Basic Research Program of China (No. 2011CB707703), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21322507, 201175072, 51173083), the National Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (No. 12JCYBJC13300), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 63140001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at.
